questions.stem: A 12-year-old overweight boy presents with a one-week history of left hip pain and limping. The pain is worse with weight-bearing and he has been limping for three days. On examination, the left hip has limited internal rotation and he prefers to hold the hip in external rotation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic presentation of an overweight adolescent with antalgic gait, limited internal rotation, and external rotation posture is typical of SUFE. Perthes' disease typically occurs in younger children (4-8 years) and presents with a painless limp. Septic arthritis would cause severe pain, fever, and refusal to weight bear. Transient synovitis is usually post-infective and less severe. Femoral neck fracture is rare without significant trauma.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with a two-month history of right hip pain that is worse with physical activity. She has a mild limp. On examination, there is slight limitation of internal rotation and some hip flexion contracture. Radiographs show a mild slip of the proximal femoral epiphysis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Chronic SUFE presents with progressive hip pain and limp over weeks to months. The radiograph confirms the slip. Perthes' disease would show femoral head fragmentation and flattening. DDH is usually detected in infancy. Coxa vara is a different deformity, often congenital. Osteoid osteoma causes night pain relieved by NSAIDs and would show a lucent nidus on imaging.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe right hip pain after a minor fall while playing football. He is unable to weight bear. On examination, the right leg is shortened and externally rotated. He has tenderness over the hip joint. Radiograph shows a displaced proximal femoral epiphysis with obvious slip. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
questions.explanation: Acute-on-chronic SUFE occurs when a chronic slip suddenly displaces after minor trauma, presenting with severe pain and inability to weight bear. Septic arthritis would have systemic symptoms and joint aspiration findings. Femoral neck fracture is rare in this age group without major trauma. Perthes' disease is more gradual. Avascular necrosis would have preceding risk factors and different imaging.

questions.stem: An 11-year-old obese boy presents with a three-week history of right knee pain and a limp. There is no history of trauma. He has no hip pain. On examination, there is mild swelling around the knee but no effusion or focal tenderness. Hip range of motion is limited in internal rotation and abduction. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Referred pain to the knee from hip pathology is a classic and easily-missed presentation of SUFE. An obese adolescent with knee pain and a limp requires hip examination and imaging. Patellofemoral pain and Osgood-Schlatter are common but would have focal knee findings. Septic knee would be acute and severe. Perthes' disease is less likely given age and obesity.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy who was treated with in situ pinning for a left SUFE three months ago now presents with increasing left hip pain and stiffness. He has difficulty walking and reports a grating sensation. On examination, the left hip has reduced range of motion in all planes and pain at extremes. Radiographs show joint space narrowing and no evidence of avascular necrosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
questions.explanation: Chondrolysis is a rare complication of SUFE, presenting with progressive pain, stiffness, and joint space narrowing after treatment. Avascular necrosis would show femoral head collapse and sclerosis. Septic arthritis would have systemic signs. Re-slip is unlikely after fixation. Perthes' disease is not related to prior SUFE.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy presents with a 3-month history of right hip pain and a limp. On examination, he has limited internal rotation of the hip. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Plain radiograph of the pelvis with frog-leg lateral views
Plain radiograph of the right hip (AP view only)
questions.explanation: SUFE is best diagnosed on plain radiographs; the frog-leg lateral view is essential to detect subtle slips. An AP view alone may miss mild slips. MRI is not first-line and is reserved for cases with high suspicion but normal X-rays.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl presents with left hip pain for 4 weeks. Initial plain radiographs (AP pelvis and frog-leg lateral) are reported as normal. However, her symptoms and examination are strongly suggestive of SUFE. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: When X-rays are normal but clinical suspicion is high, MRI is the test of choice to detect early slipped epiphysis, showing bone marrow oedema and subtle slip. Repeat X-rays may demonstrate progression but delay diagnosis. Ultrasound and bone scan are less specific.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy has a confirmed diagnosis of SUFE. On the frog-leg lateral view, the proximal femoral epiphysis is displaced posteriorly relative to the metaphysis. The angle measured between the epiphysis and the femoral neck is 25 degrees. Which of the following best describes this finding?
questions.explanation: SUFE is classified by slip angle: mild (<30°), moderate (30–50°), severe (>50°). An angle of 25° is therefore a mild slip.

questions.stem: On a frog-leg lateral radiograph of a 13-year-old with hip pain, a line drawn along the superior border of the femoral neck fails to intersect the femoral epiphysis. This finding is known as:
questions.explanation: Klein's line is the line drawn along the superior femoral neck; when it does not intersect the epiphysis, this is Trethowan's sign, indicative of SUFE. Steel's sign is a metaphyseal blanch, and Shenton's line is an intact arc of the obturator foramen and femoral neck.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl presents with acute onset of severe left hip pain after a fall while playing. She cannot bear weight. On examination, the left leg is held in external rotation and shortening. Plain radiographs of the pelvis show a break in Shenton's line and a crescent-shaped radiodensity at the metaphysis (Steel's sign). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of Shenton's line disruption and Steel's sign (metaphyseal blanch) is classic for SUFE. Perthes shows fragmentation and collapse, septic arthritis has joint space widening, and fracture usually has a clear break.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy presents with a 2-month history of right hip and knee pain. He can weight-bear. X-ray shows a mild SUFE (slip angle <30°). What is the definitive first-line treatment?
options: Prophylactic pinning of the contralateral hip
questions.explanation: For a stable, mild SUFE, in situ pinning is the standard definitive treatment to prevent further slippage and avoid complications such as avascular necrosis. Observation or physiotherapy alone are not sufficient, as the slip will progress. Prophylactic pinning of the contralateral hip is not routinely indicated in the absence of risk factors. Open reduction is reserved for severe displacement or failed in situ fixation.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy with known hypothyroidism (on thyroxine) presents with hip pain. X-ray confirms a stable SUFE. What is the most appropriate management?
options: In situ pinning and thyroxine optimisation
questions.explanation: Surgical stabilisation with in situ pinning is the definitive treatment for SUFE, regardless of associated endocrinopathies. While thyroxine optimisation is important for overall health, it does not replace the need for immediate surgical fixation. Observation or non-operative management would allow progression of the slip.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old obese boy has a unilateral SUFE treated with in situ pinning. His contralateral hip is asymptomatic and X-ray shows no slip. He has no other risk factors. What is the most appropriate management for the contralateral hip?
options: MRI to assess for preclinical slip
questions.explanation: In the absence of risk factors (e.g., endocrine disorders, significant obesity), routine prophylactic pinning of the contralateral hip is not recommended by NICE due to potential complications. Surveillance is an option, but the question asks for the most appropriate management, and 'no active intervention' is the guideline-recommended approach.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy presents with acute SUFE. Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED as first-line management?
questions.explanation: Closed manipulation under anaesthesia is contraindicated in acute SUFE because it increases the risk of avascular necrosis by disrupting the tenuous blood supply to the femoral head. In situ pinning is the standard treatment. Traction, spica, and NSAIDs are not definitive and are not first-line, but are not contraindicated per se.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy presents to the ED with a two-week history of left-sided limp and vague knee pain. On examination, the left hip has limited internal rotation and he prefers to keep the leg externally rotated. Observations are normal. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Request an anteroposterior and frog-leg lateral radiograph of the left hip
Obtain an urgent MRI scan of the left hip
Reassure the patient and arrange outpatient review in 2 weeks
Perform an ultrasound scan of the left hip
Check full blood count, ESR and CRP
questions.explanation: In a child with limp and knee pain, SUFE must be excluded. Plain radiographs (AP and frog-leg lateral) are the first-line imaging to detect the slip. MRI is more sensitive but not first step; bloods may be normal; reassurance is inappropriate given the presentation.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with acute-onset right hip pain after a minor fall. She can weight-bear but with a limp. Anteroposterior and frog-leg lateral radiographs confirm a mild stable SUFE (slip angle <30°). What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange urgent orthopaedic referral for in situ surgical fixation
Apply skin traction and admit for bed rest
Obtain an MRI of the right hip to assess for avascular necrosis
Discharge with crutches and a follow-up appointment in 6 weeks
Prescribe a course of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
questions.explanation: Even stable SUFE requires surgical pinning to prevent progression and avascular necrosis. Urgent orthopaedic referral is the correct next action; traction and discharge are inappropriate, and MRI is not needed initially.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old obese boy presents to the ED with acute, severe right hip pain after a stumble. He cannot bear weight and holds the leg externally rotated. Radiographs show a severe SUFE with slip angle >40°. He rates his pain as 9/10. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Request a CT scan of the hip
Attempt closed reduction under sedation in the ED
Obtain an urgent MRI to evaluate for avascular necrosis
Apply skin traction to the right leg
questions.explanation: In acute severe pain, immediate analgesia (IV morphine) is the priority. Closed reduction is not performed in ED; MRI or CT would delay treatment. Traction is not first-line. After analgesia, urgent orthopaedic referral is needed.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy with known bilateral stable SUFE (untreated for 3 months) presents with sudden worsening of right hip pain and inability to move the leg. Radiographs show progression of slip and possible avascular necrosis. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Provide analgesia and refer for physiotherapy
Refer for immediate surgical reduction and fixation
Start oral bisphosphonates for avascular necrosis
Consult rheumatology for possible inflammatory arthritis
Arrange an urgent MRI of the right hip to confirm avascular necrosis
questions.explanation: Acute deterioration with suspected avascular necrosis requires urgent surgical stabilisation to prevent further progression. MRI can be considered but should not delay surgery. Bisphosphonates, physiotherapy, and rheumatology referral are not first-line.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy, on growth hormone therapy for idiopathic short stature, presents with a six-week history of bilateral hip pain and a waddling gait. Radiographs show bilateral SUFE with mild slips. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe a course of physiotherapy and review in 3 months
Start bisphosphonate therapy to prevent avascular necrosis
Arrange urgent bilateral in situ surgical fixation
Order a bone scan to assess for other skeletal abnormalities
Refer to endocrinology for growth hormone dose adjustment
questions.explanation: Bilateral SUFE, especially in an endocrine setting (growth hormone therapy), requires urgent surgical pinning to prevent progression. Endocrine referral can follow, but treatment is the priority. Bisphosphonates, physiotherapy, and bone scans are not appropriate next steps.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy presents with acute onset of severe left hip pain after a fall. He cannot bear weight on the leg. On examination, the left leg is held in external rotation and there is pain on attempted internal rotation. Which single finding is most concerning for an unstable (acute) slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE)?
questions.explanation: Inability to bear weight indicates an unstable slip, which requires urgent orthopaedic intervention. External rotation can occur in chronic SUFE as well, but inability to bear weight is a red flag for acute instability.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with persistent hip pain and stiffness 6 months after in situ pinning for SUFE. She has limited range of motion in all planes, especially internal rotation. Which complication of SUFE is most likely?
questions.explanation: Chondrolysis is characterized by progressive loss of joint space and stiffness after SUFE treatment. Avascular necrosis would present more acutely with severe pain and inability to bear weight, not just insidious stiffness.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old child presents with acute hip pain and fever. He is unable to move his hip due to pain. Which single finding would most strongly suggest septic arthritis rather than SUFE?
options: Obligatory external rotation on hip flexion
questions.explanation: High fever and rigors are systemic infection signs classic for septic arthritis, which is a medical emergency. SUFE rarely causes high temperature or rigors. Obligatory external rotation is characteristic of SUFE.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy with known hypothyroidism on medication presents with bilateral hip pain and a waddling gait. Which finding is most concerning for bilateral SUFE?
options: Inability to internally rotate hips beyond neutral
questions.explanation: Loss of internal rotation beyond neutral is an early, sensitive sign of SUFE and bilateral involvement is common in endocrine disorders. External rotation may be subtle; inability to internally rotate beyond neutral is a classic red flag.

questions.stem: A child with known chronic SUFE (stable) suddenly experiences severe hip pain and is unable to bear weight after a minor stumble. What does this acute deterioration most likely represent?
questions.explanation: Acute-on-chronic slip occurs when a stable chronic SUFE becomes unstable after minor trauma, causing acute pain and inability to bear weight. This is an orthopaedic emergency. Avascular necrosis is less common without surgery.
